Volunteer: it starts ~15–45 minutes after eating, your face/neck get hot and red, your heart pounds/races, and it settles in 1–2 hours if you rest and drink water.

If asked, add: mild headache, tingling/burning around mouth/face, slight shortness of breath, nausea/upset stomach, worse with Chinese food, flavored chips, instant soups, seasoning mixes; usually no hives but itchy/warm skin; Benadryl helped once but not always.

Withhold unless specifically asked: stress/caffeine makes it worse, occasional throat tightness but you can talk/swallow, fast breathing/lightheadedness, avoiding exercise after meals, higher blood sugars after these meals.

Resist admitting at first: panic/“might die,” skipping social meals, taking extra OTC allergy pills/antacids secretly.

Mention misconceptions: you think MSG is the trigger and fear you must buy expensive “clean label” foods, custom restaurant meals, and detox/supplements.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Monosodium glutamate (MSG) sensitivity (MSG symptom complex)
acceptable_alternatives: Food additive intolerance (non-IgE mediated) to MSG
required_evidence: Reproducible, short-latency symptoms occurring after ingestion of MSG-containing foods (e.g., restaurant/processed foods)
Symptoms are self-limited and resolve with avoidance; no progressive course
Absence of objective features of IgE-mediated allergy (e.g., anaphylaxis, wheeze/bronchospasm, angioedema) during episodes
No clinical evidence supporting primary cardiopulmonary disease (e.g., persistent exertional chest pain, syncope, resting hypoxemia, progressive dyspnea) to explain episodes
Improvement with elimination and recurrence with re-challenge (history-based; formal blinded challenge optional)

expected_lab_results.Capillary blood glucose (fingerstick): Typically normal for acute MSG-related symptoms; check to exclude hypoglycemia if symptomatic
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Expected within normal range unless comorbid diabetes is present (not required for diagnosis)

medications: Cetirizine 10 mg PO once daily as needed for hives/itching if they occur with episodes
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for headache (max 3000 mg/day)
non_pharmacological: Primary management: avoid MSG and high-free-glutamate foods; read labels and ask about seasonings/sauces (e.g., hydrolyzed vegetable protein, autolyzed yeast extract, flavor enhancers)
Keep a symptom and food diary; consider structured elimination (2–4 weeks) followed by cautious re-introduction to confirm trigger
If symptoms include flushing/palpitations/anxiety, use supportive care (hydration, rest) and avoid triggers such as large bolus exposures
If episodes include chest pain, syncope, persistent dyspnea, or objective wheeze/angioedema, seek urgent evaluation to exclude cardiopulmonary disease or true allergy
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Follow up with primary care in 2–4 weeks to review diary and response to avoidance; earlier if severe or escalating symptoms

must_include: MSG sensitivity is typically an intolerance/symptom complex and is not the same as a true IgE-mediated food allergy
Diagnosis is clinical and based on reproducible symptoms after exposure and improvement with avoidance
Review red flags that would suggest alternative diagnoses needing urgent workup (persistent chest pain, syncope, progressive dyspnea, hypoxemia, angioedema, wheeze)
Avoidance of MSG-containing foods is the main treatment
Symptomatic medications are optional and targeted (e.g., antihistamine for hives, acetaminophen for headache)
When to seek emergency care (trouble breathing, swelling of lips/tongue/throat, fainting, severe chest pain)
Acknowledge prior allergies (penicillin, shellfish) and clarify that MSG sensitivity is separate from shellfish allergy
Discuss uncertainty and plan to confirm trigger with an elimination/re-challenge approach
Shared decision-making about whether allergy referral is needed (e.g., if recurrent hives/angioedema or unclear trigger)
Label-reading and common hidden sources of MSG/free glutamate
Strategies for eating out (ask about seasoning blends, soups, sauces, bouillon, flavor enhancers)
Keeping a diary to identify dose-response and co-triggers (alcohol, stress, large meals)

Formulate a working diagnosis of MSG sensitivity/intolerance based on clinical pattern and absence of anaphylaxis/red flags; include differential (food allergy, asthma, panic, reflux, migraine, histamine intolerance).
Explain diagnosis (intolerance vs IgE allergy), clinical basis, and red flags requiring urgent evaluation.
Explain treatment plan: avoidance as mainstay, optional symptom-directed meds, and clear emergency precautions.

Address concerns: acknowledge penicillin/shellfish allergies, clarify MSG is separate from shellfish allergy, discuss uncertainty and elimination/re-challenge plan, and shared decision about allergy referral if recurrent hives/angioedema or unclear trigger.

If prescribing symptomatic medication, check for drug-drug interactions with current meds (non-critical but expected when applicable).

Arrange follow-up to review diary, response to avoidance, and consider referral/testing if symptoms persist or are atypical.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and plan.

Start by volunteering: tight/heavy pressure in the middle of your chest with walking or stairs that eases after a few minutes of rest, new shortness of breath with exertion, and unusual fatigue for weeks. If asked directly, admit it can spread to left shoulder/jaw with sweating and mild nausea; worse in cold air/stress; occasional brief “fluttering”; need two pillows to sleep; evening ankle puffiness; declining exercise tolerance over 2–3 months; one parent had a heart attack in their 40s (hesitate and downplay due to insurance worries). correct_diagnosis.primary: Autosomal dominant familial hypercholesterolemia with premature coronary artery disease (CAD)
acceptable_alternatives: Heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia with established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease
Premature atherosclerotic coronary artery disease due to inherited dyslipidemia (familial hypercholesterolemia phenotype)
required_evidence: Markedly elevated LDL-C and/or Apolipoprotein B consistent with familial hypercholesterolemia phenotype
Elevated Lipoprotein(a) as an additional inherited ASCVD risk enhancer (if present)

expected_lab_results.Troponin I (high-sensitivity): If acute coronary syndrome is present: elevated above 99th percentile with a rising/falling pattern on serial testing; if stable CAD: normal
expected_lab_results.CK-MB: If acute myocardial injury: elevated with dynamic change; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.Total cholesterol: Elevated (often >300 mg/dL in untreated familial hypercholesterolemia)
expected_lab_results.LDL-C: Severely elevated (often >=190 mg/dL untreated; may remain elevated despite partial therapy)
expected_lab_results.HDL-C: Often normal to low; not diagnostic
expected_lab_results.Triglycerides: Usually normal to mildly elevated (familial hypercholesterolemia typically not severe hypertriglyceridemia)
expected_lab_results.Lipoprotein(a): May be elevated (e.g., >=50 mg/dL or >=125 nmol/L), increasing ASCVD risk
expected_lab_results.Apolipoprotein B: Elevated (reflecting high atherogenic particle burden; commonly >130 mg/dL untreated)

medications: Atorvastatin 80 mg PO daily

Start with: “I keep having weird spells where my vision goes funny and I get confused for a few minutes.” Volunteer: both-side shimmering/blank edges of vision, then confusion and trouble getting words out for a few minutes; sometimes a bad headache follows with nausea and wanting lights off; spells increasing over months. Clinical correlation with seizures and/or neurodevelopmental, visual-spatial, or cognitive symptoms consistent with parieto-occipital involvement
Exclusion of acute metabolic derangements as primary cause of events (basic electrolytes, glucose if available)

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically normal (no leukocytosis expected unless intercurrent infection)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: Typically normal (hyponatremia would be a red flag for seizure provocation or medication effect if on ASMs)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Bicarbonate (CO2): Typically normal; may be low if recent generalized tonic-clonic seizure with lactic acidosis (transient)
expected_lab_results.CMP - BUN: Typically normal (assess renal function context for medication selection)

non_pharmacological: Neurology referral for epilepsy evaluation and longitudinal management

Seizure safety counseling (no driving until cleared per local law, avoid swimming/bathing alone, heights, operating heavy machinery)
Discuss rescue plan for prolonged seizures (e.g., prescribe intranasal midazolam or rectal diazepam if history of prolonged seizures/status; train caregivers if applicable)
Screen for and address comorbidities common in cortical malformations (developmental/cognitive assessment, vision assessment given parieto-occipital involvement)
Consider genetic counseling/testing and prenatal/perinatal history review when appropriate to etiology evaluation

must_include: Polymicrogyria is a congenital cortical development malformation (not contagious, not cancer)
Location (bilateral parasagittal parieto-occipital) can be associated with seizures and visual-spatial/cognitive symptoms
Symptoms can vary; condition is structural and often lifelong, with management focused on seizure control and supportive therapies
What findings support the diagnosis (MRI description) and what additional tests may help (EEG)
Goal is seizure prevention and reduction of seizure-related injury
How to take levetiracetam (schedule, titration plan, do not stop abruptly)
Common adverse effects (somnolence, dizziness, irritability/mood changes) and when to seek help (severe mood/behavior changes, rash, suicidality)
When to call emergency services (seizure >5 minutes, repeated seizures without recovery, breathing problems, serious injury)
Discuss prognosis: many achieve seizure control with medication, but some have refractory epilepsy; surgery candidacy depends on seizure focus and eloquent cortex involvement
Review safety restrictions (driving, water safety, work/school accommodations)
Acknowledge psychosocial impact and offer resources (epilepsy foundation/support groups)
Sleep hygiene and adherence to antiseizure medication
Avoid seizure triggers where relevant (sleep deprivation, alcohol excess, missed doses)
Counsel on medication interactions with alcohol/sedatives and importance of informing clinicians of all OTC/herbal products
Discuss contraception/pregnancy planning if applicable (preconception counseling and folic acid; coordinate with neurology/OB for ASM choice)

Initiate levetiracetam with clear schedule and titration, renal-adjusted if needed; warn not to stop abruptly; provide rescue/emergency guidance when appropriate.

Explain treatment milestone: goals (seizure prevention/injury reduction), how to take levetiracetam, common adverse effects and danger signs (severe mood/behavior change, rash, suicidality), and emergency criteria (seizure >5 min, clusters without recovery, breathing problems, serious injury).

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 24-year-old anxious but clear-speaking elementary school teacher. Start with: “I can’t get this bleeding to stop after a tooth pull.” Volunteer that it seemed okay at first, then a few hours later it started bleeding heavily again; you feel tired and a bit lightheaded today; and you bruise very easily. If asked about bleeding history, share long nosebleeds, cuts that restart bleeding later, worse-than-expected bleeding after stitches/surgeries, gum bleeding with brushing/flossing, large painful bruises/lumps after minor injuries, and that your umbilical bleeding as a baby took a long time to stop. Do not mention deep muscle swelling after bumps, headaches after minor head hits, wounds reopening 1–2 days later, family “bleeding problems,” or miscarriages unless specifically prompted. Resist discussing extremely heavy periods, bleeding after sex, and avoiding sports/dating; only admit if asked gently and privately. Express misconceptions: “My PT/INR and PTT were normal, so I didn’t think it could be a bleeding disorder,” and wonder about platelets or vitamin K. Voice cost worries about factor, preventive infusions, and home supplies. If the clinician shows empathy, relax slightly, thank them, and share more details.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Community-acquired pneumonia (CAP), outpatient-appropriate (non-severe) pending vitals and imaging
acceptable_alternatives: Influenza or other viral upper respiratory infection

required_evidence: Acute cough plus at least one of: fever, pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea, focal lung findings (crackles/bronchial breath sounds), or hypoxia
No immediate features of severe pneumonia (e.g., hypotension, confusion, respiratory distress) or need for ICU-level care
CBC supportive of infection possible (WBC elevated with neutrophilia) but can be normal
Electrolytes without alternative primary explanation for symptoms; assess for dehydration-related changes
Penicillin allergy history documented (hives as a child) to guide antibiotic choice

expected_lab_results.WBC: Often mildly to moderately elevated (e.g., 11-18 x10^9/L) but may be normal early or in viral disease

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Typically normal; anemia not expected as primary driver
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Normal or mildly elevated as acute-phase reactant
expected_lab_results.Sodium: May be mildly low (SIADH/dehydration) or normal

expected_lab_results.Chloride: Usually normal; may track with sodium changes
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Often normal; may be low with hyperventilation/metabolic acidosis from sepsis/dehydration

correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess (telehealth or in-person) in 48-72 hours, sooner if worsening; ensure clinical improvement by day 3

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for acute respiratory infection/CAP risk stratification and determine need for urgent escalation (onset, fever, cough/sputum, dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, sick contacts, comorbidities, pregnancy, immunocompromise, smoking/vaping, baseline function).

Obtain objective severity/diagnostic data when available/appropriate: vitals including oxygen saturation; consider chest X-ray to confirm pneumonia; consider viral testing (COVID/flu) if it changes management; consider CBC/BMP if moderate/severe or comorbid.
Use returned vitals/SpO2 and imaging/labs to support working diagnosis (CAP vs viral), assess severity, and determine outpatient vs ED/hospital evaluation.

Non-critical safety step before prescribing: screen current meds/supplements for interactions relevant to chosen therapy (e.g., doxycycline with iron/calcium/antacids; anticoagulants).

If outpatient CAP is reasonable: choose an antibiotic consistent with penicillin allergy history (e.g., doxycycline) and provide correct dose/duration; if viral more likely and no bacterial features, avoid antibiotics and focus on supportive care.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis and need for vitals/SpO2 and possible CXR; explain treatment (how to take doxycycline, precautions, symptom control, expected improvement in 48–72h); address concerns (allergy details, side effects, when to stop/seek help, red flags); lifestyle and infection control; vaccine counseling after recovery.
Arrange follow-up and contingency plan: reassessment at 48–72 hours or sooner if worse; instructions for urgent evaluation based on red flags or abnormal vitals/SpO2.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, providing return precautions, and documenting plan.

No focal peritoneal abdominal findings or red flags suggesting surgical abdomen (or, if present, triggers escalation rather than this diagnosis)

expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (stress leukocytosis possible)
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Normal to mildly low (can be low with GI losses and free-water intake) or mildly high if severe dehydration
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Low to normal (GI losses may cause hypokalemia)
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Low (non-anion gap metabolic acidosis from diarrhea) OR mildly high if predominant vomiting (metabolic alkalosis); interpret with symptoms
expected_lab_results.BUN: Elevated relative to baseline (pre-renal azotemia from dehydration)

If IV needed: 0.9% sodium chloride 1 L IV bolus, reassess; repeat as needed based on perfusion/urine output
Diet: advance as tolerated; avoid alcohol, high-fat foods; continue bland foods; maintain hydration
Return precautions: worsening abdominal pain, persistent fever, blood in stool, inability to keep fluids down, syncope, confusion, decreased urination, symptoms >72 hours
Infectious precautions: hand hygiene; avoid preparing food for others while symptomatic
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 24-48 hours (sooner same day/ED if unable to maintain hydration or red flags develop)

required_evidence: Compatible acute illness (e.g., low-grade fever, maculopapular rash, arthralgia, nonpurulent conjunctivitis, headache, myalgia) with recent travel to or residence in an area with Zika transmission or unprotected sex with a potentially exposed partner

If pregnant or planning pregnancy: pregnancy status assessed and fetal risk counseling initiated
Diagnostic testing plan documented when indicated (NAAT/RT-PCR on serum/urine early; serology with cross-reactivity caveats)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Usually normal; mild thrombocytopenia can occur (marked thrombocytopenia suggests dengue more than Zika)

non_pharmacological: Supportive care: rest and oral hydration
Avoid NSAIDs and aspirin until dengue is reasonably excluded due to bleeding risk
Prevent mosquito exposure for at least 3 weeks after symptom onset (repellent, long sleeves, bed nets) to reduce onward transmission

Counsel on sexual transmission prevention: use condoms/abstain; if male exposed/symptomatic use condoms for 3 months; if female exposed/symptomatic use condoms for 2 months (or follow local public health guidance)

correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 48-72 hours (earlier if worsening); immediate evaluation for red flags (severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, mucosal bleeding, lethargy/confusion, dyspnea, dehydration, pregnancy concerns, neurologic symptoms such as weakness/paresthesias suggestive of Guillain-Barré syndrome).

must_include: Zika is usually a mild, self-limited viral illness; many people have mild symptoms or none
How it is acquired (mosquito-borne; can also be sexually transmitted)
Need to consider and/or test for dengue/chikungunya because early symptoms overlap and management differs (NSAID avoidance until dengue excluded)
Treatment is supportive (fluids, rest, acetaminophen for fever/pain)
Avoid aspirin/NSAIDs until dengue is excluded; avoid dehydration

Discuss sexual transmission precautions and duration
Set expectations for symptom duration (typically several days to ~1 week; fatigue may last longer)

Hydration and rest; avoid alcohol excess if liver enzymes elevated

Synthesize exposure + clinical syndrome + labs into diagnosis (suspected or confirmed Zika) and explicitly address differential (dengue/chikungunya) and uncertainty if pending.

Provide supportive medications only (e.g., acetaminophen with safe dosing); avoid aspirin/NSAIDs until dengue is excluded; avoid unnecessary antivirals/antibiotics.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy), autosomal recessive subtype (e.g., CMT4)
acceptable_alternatives: Hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy (HMSN), autosomal recessive
Diabetic distal symmetric polyneuropathy (if objective evidence of diabetes and neuropathy pattern fits)
Vitamin B12 deficiency neuropathy (if B12/MMA abnormal)
required_evidence: Progressive length-dependent peripheral neuropathy pattern (distal weakness/atrophy, sensory loss, reduced/absent reflexes), typically starting in childhood/adolescence
Family history consistent with autosomal recessive inheritance (affected siblings, unaffected parents, consanguinity possible)
Electrodiagnostic confirmation (NCS/EMG) showing hereditary neuropathy pattern (demyelinating with slowed conduction velocities or axonal with reduced amplitudes depending on subtype)
Exclusion of common treatable mimics using available labs (B12/MMA, TSH, diabetes testing, inflammatory markers, CK)
Consider genetic testing to confirm subtype after neuromuscular evaluation

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: May be elevated if patient has diabetes or prediabetes; can be normal in CMT (not required for diagnosis). If maternal diabetes only, patient A1c often normal.
expected_lab_results.Fasting plasma glucose: May be elevated if patient has diabetes/prediabetes; can be normal in CMT.
expected_lab_results.Vitamin B12: Typically normal (low would suggest alternative diagnosis).
expected_lab_results.Methylmalonic acid: Typically normal (elevated would support functional B12 deficiency).
expected_lab_results.TSH: Typically normal (abnormal would suggest hypothyroid-related neuropathy).
expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): Usually normal or mildly elevated; marked elevation suggests myopathy rather than CMT.
expected_lab_results.ESR: Typically normal (elevation would suggest inflammatory/vasculitic process).
expected_lab_results.CRP: Typically normal (elevation would suggest inflammatory/vasculitic process).

medications: No disease-modifying pharmacotherapy indicated for CMT; treat neuropathic pain if present: gabapentin 100-300 mg PO at bedtime, titrate every 3-7 days as tolerated to effect
Alternative for neuropathic pain if needed: duloxetine 30 mg PO daily, increase to 60 mg PO daily after 1-2 weeks as tolerated
non_pharmacological: Refer to neurology/neuromuscular clinic for confirmation with NCS/EMG and consideration of genetic testing/counseling
Physical therapy for strengthening, stretching, balance and gait training; occupational therapy for hand function/adaptive devices
Ankle-foot orthoses (AFOs) for foot drop; consider custom orthotics; assess for scoliosis and foot deformities (pes cavus/hammer toes) with referral to orthopedics/podiatry as needed
Fall-risk assessment and home safety interventions; assistive devices (cane/walker) if indicated
Avoid/limit exposure to known neurotoxic medications when possible (e.g., vincristine; caution with others per neuromuscular guidance)
Screen for diabetes in the patient given maternal diabetes history; optimize glycemic control if abnormal to reduce superimposed neuropathy risk
Genetic counseling for patient/family regarding autosomal recessive inheritance, carrier status, and reproductive options
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-8 weeks for symptom control, function, and completion of neurology referral/testing; sooner (1-2 weeks) if starting/titrating neuropathic pain medication or if falls/rapid progression occur.

must_include: CMT is a hereditary peripheral neuropathy affecting motor and sensory nerves, typically slowly progressive
Autosomal recessive inheritance means both parents are usually carriers; siblings may be affected; offer genetic counseling
Labs are used to rule out treatable causes that can mimic or worsen neuropathy (B12 deficiency, thyroid disease, diabetes, inflammation)
Confirmatory testing usually includes nerve conduction studies/EMG and possibly genetic testing
No cure currently; goals are maintaining mobility/function, preventing complications, and treating symptoms (pain, cramps, foot drop)
Core management includes PT/OT, orthotics (AFOs), and fall prevention; consider orthopedic/podiatry input for deformities
If neuropathic pain medication is used, discuss expected benefits, common side effects (sedation/dizziness for gabapentin; nausea/sleep changes for duloxetine), and titration plan
Discuss prognosis as typically gradual progression with variable severity; emphasize monitoring for functional changes and falls
Explain that diabetes (if present) can worsen neuropathy; recommend screening/management given maternal diabetes history
Review allergy history (penicillin urticarial rash; latex contact dermatitis) and how it affects care (avoid latex products; document antibiotic allergy)
Foot care and footwear guidance to prevent ulcers/injury, especially if sensory loss
Regular low-impact exercise within tolerance and stretching to maintain strength and flexibility
Home safety measures to reduce falls (lighting, removing tripping hazards) and when to use assistive devices

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for suspected peripheral neuropathy/CMT: onset and progression, distribution, weakness vs sensory symptoms, gait/falls, cramps/pain, functional impact, family history, prior evaluations, comorbidities (diabetes/thyroid), medications/toxins, red flags.

Critical safety check before placing any orders that could involve allergens (e.g., latex-containing supplies, contrast agents, meds). Confirm/document penicillin urticarial rash and latex contact dermatitis; ensure latex avoidance.
Order screening labs to rule out treatable causes/mimics or contributors to neuropathy (e.g., A1c/fasting glucose, B12 +/- MMA, TSH, CMP, CBC; consider ESR/CRP, SPEP/IFE if clinically indicated).
Review lab results to exclude reversible etiologies or identify contributors (e.g., diabetes, B12 deficiency, thyroid disease) that may mimic or worsen neuropathy.
Synthesize clinical picture and results; state likely CMT (hereditary peripheral neuropathy) and the need for confirmatory testing (NCS/EMG; consider genetic testing).

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (hereditary, slowly progressive; autosomal recessive counseling; role of labs; confirmatory NCS/EMG/genetics), explain treatment (no cure; PT/OT, orthotics/AFOs, fall prevention, ortho/podiatry; neuropathic pain options and side effects/titration), address concerns (prognosis; diabetes screening/management given family history; allergy/latex implications), lifestyle (foot care, exercise/stretching, home safety).
Critical safety check before any prescription; confirm and document allergies and ensure chosen meds are safe given allergy profile and latex avoidance in dispensing/administration context.
Non-critical interaction screen prior to prescribing neuropathic pain medications or other agents; verify no significant interactions with current meds/substances.

If symptomatic neuropathic pain/cramps present and patient desires treatment, prescribe an evidence-based option (e.g., gabapentin or duloxetine) with titration plan and counseling on common adverse effects; otherwise document shared decision to defer meds.
Arrange follow-up to review symptom trajectory, falls, function, lab results if pending, and referrals/testing (NCS/EMG, genetics, PT/OT, orthotics, podiatry/orthopedics). Provide return precautions.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, answering questions, and ensuring next steps and safety plan.
